title: Introduce a new signed Windows installer with automated build process
body:
Resolves #14224 

## Overview

- Automated eager WSL installation as part of the installer with less steps than the current deferred on-demand installation
- Provides a professional/polished UI with Podman branding (Wasn't an option before with msitools)
- Separate Win build process signs the installer and nested components, along with podman itself 
    - Minimizes auth nags when downloading and running since content is trusted
    - Reduces false alarms / severity score in virus scanners  (e.g. #13415, https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/15133)
    - Helps with group policies that restrict untrusted code
- Adds win-sshproxy.exe to the zip distro both to support the build and to satisfy a community ask (requested a few times in different placese.g #13675) 
- Introduces an automated win binary release process using a Github Action, along with support for GUI/CLI manual launching (see RELEASE_PROCESS.md changes)
- Supports Podman Desktop experience 
- Adds a PR/build task to Cirrus CI to verify the installer builds and runs correctly with future changes

## Why a separate Windows build chain?
- Cloud Vault based Windows signing toolchain only exists on Windows today - we would have to recreate that
- msitools does not (yet) provide the full functionality available in Wix which is needed to achieve the above items (e.g. no burn boostrap, no full UI)

## Added Cmds and purpose (only built as part of Windows installer build chain)

- podman-msihooks 
    - Generates a DLL to extend the internal MSI (wrapped by the burn bootrap) for aspects around WSL detection
- podman-wslkerninst 
    - Uses by the installer to launch installation of the WSL Linux kernel

## IMPORTANT LICENSING NOTE

Three XML files in this PR are modifications to the provided templates in WiX and are licensed under different yet compatible terms, the MS-RL (noted in source files). MS-RL is a weak copyleft license (weaker than LGPL and GPL + CP) that only governs the file itself - not viral, so fine to include in projects under different terms (e.g. AL in this case). Further these are data files, so never linked to podman code, only read as input. 

- contrib/win-installer/podman-theme.wxl
- contrib/win-installer/podman-theme.xml
- contrib/win-installer/podman-ui.wxs


## Release Process

As noted in the RELEASE_PROCESS.md diff, generating the Windows installer is largely hands off and integrates with the existing release approach. The automated job looks at published and edited releases to see if they contain client windows zips (needed for repackaging), and then adds the installer to the release (also updates shasums).

## Other Notes

- MSI and winpath are obviated by this change, but left behind in source so they are still available to users to build if needed. The intention would be to remove in a future release. If preferred I could update the PR to remove them now.

## Preview Image

![podman-inst-action](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/273664/188041592-d177d6a3-3245-45a1-8a95-b960916dd739.gif)

[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]

```release-note
Introduced a new Windows installer (podman-x.y.z-setup.exe) that replaces the existing msi
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *n1hility*, *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Release process changes LGTM, but I don't know enough about Windows to review the rest

> [NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]

Not having looked at the changes yet, the need for this magic bothers me slightly for such a large & significant feature.:

1. It would be good to test the installer functions at PR time.  The worst possible time to find out the installer is broken is when a new release is tagged, we need to know as early as possible.  We can run [Windows VMs](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/images/os-details#windows_client) and [containers](https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/windows/) in CI, it's not been done to date, b/c we (in general) lack the expertise.  Maybe this is something you'd consider helping with?
2. If there are file additions which cannot practically be tested, please update `contrib/cirrus/pr-should-include-tests` to ignore them.  Otherwise anybody that touches them will again need the `[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]` magic.

After an initial look, I'm happy to see this all come together and believe it will be a great benefit to the community.  Thanks for taking this work on.  I like how you've engineered an "anti-woopsie" button into the workflow, where things can be twiddled with after a release tag is pushed and/or if artifacts are manually built and uploaded.  

My main concerns are relating to the longevity of the automation, especially the batch scripts.  The design of github-actions doesn't make this easy, always running the workflow from main but using scripts from the target (tag or branch) with no way to really test changes in a PR before finding out the run will break as soon as a tag is pushed.  At the same time, moving this to CIrrus-CI raises some certificate/key/signing safety concerns.  Maybe there's a way to do it with a key-store in GCE similar to azure (I don't know)?  The biggest reason to have this in Cirrus (IMHO) is team familiarity and that operational changes can be easily validated in a PR before a tag is pushed.  Even on an ancient release-branch.

Lastly, nobody here (besides you) has the experience to debug or troubleshoot errant Windows operations.  In the past when unfamiliar automation has been added and the expertise becomes unavailable, at the first sign of breakage it's disabled and forgotten about.  That's not your fault, but it would be a real shame given the user base that will benefit from having this working long-term.  Anyway, I don't have a good solution here, other than maybe suggesting adding lots more comments and providing links to docs for non-windows experts.

All that said, maybe this is a good starting place, and some of my long-term/longevity concerns can be addressed in followup PRs.  Mainly I just want to ensure we're going into this knowing and aware of some possible "oh crap" scenarios (not that they can/should all be solved right now).

@edsantiago i know you're not an expert on github-actions stuff either.  But I do think the workflow and the batch files are worthy of your finely-tuned eye for detail.  Would you mind giving it a once-over and a quick think?  Also please feel free to check, and tell me my longevity-fears are unfounded and I should just be quiet :grin:

> > [NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]
> 
> Not having looked at the changes yet, the need for this magic bothers me slightly for such a large & significant feature.:
> 
> 1. It would be good to test the installer functions at PR time.  The worst possible time to find out the installer is broken is when a new release is tagged, we need to know as early as possible.  We can run [Windows VMs](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/images/os-details#windows_client) and [containers](https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/windows/) in CI, it's not been done to date, b/c we (in general) lack the expertise.  Maybe this is something you'd consider helping with?
> 2. If there are file additions which cannot practically be tested, please update `contrib/cirrus/pr-should-include-tests` to ignore them.  Otherwise anybody that touches them will again need the `[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]` magic.

> > [NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]
> 
> Not having looked at the changes yet, the need for this magic bothers me slightly for such a large & significant feature.:
> 
> 1. It would be good to test the installer functions at PR time.  The worst possible time to find out the installer is broken is when a new release is tagged, we need to know as early as possible.  We can run [Windows VMs](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/images/os-details#windows_client) and [containers](https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/windows/) in CI, it's not been done to date, b/c we (in general) lack the expertise.  Maybe this is something you'd consider helping with?

I agree we should have something at PR time to verify the installer at least builds (when related installer files are touched). This is something I planned to follow-up with another change on. Unfortunately we can't (yet) test the execution of the full installer, since WSL fails without nested virt, which is not well supported on Windows with hyperscalers and cloud CI, or is cost prohibitive (e.g. GCP only supports linux with nested virt, EC2 requires you buy a high end bare metal node, GHA uses v2 non-nested nodes, etc). I have a request open with the Cirrus folks (https://github.com/cirruslabs/cirrus-ci-docs/discussions/953) to add support for direct usage of Azure VMs, which would address this problem. The same issue is also standing in the way for PR testing of podman machine on windows.  That said some basic verification is certainly doable.

Yes I am up for helping. If possible I'd like to incrementally improve on these aspects in subsequent PRs. If you prefer I could quickly turnaround and include a GHA for PR build verification temporarily. That job would then be replaced with a Cirrus task once I have the dep baseline scripted. 

> 2. If there are file additions which cannot practically be tested, please update `contrib/cirrus/pr-should-include-tests` to ignore them.  Otherwise anybody that touches them will again need the `[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]` magic.

Will do. IIRC contrib is already excluded (will double check that), but the two added commands to cmd (only used by the installer) need to exclude the full PR run.

> WSL fails without nested virt

Ugh..right, that :disappointed: 

> EC2 requires you buy a high end bare metal node

We have those!  Check out the podman_machine task in `.cirrus.yml` :grin:   In fact, maybe this is the reason to do this all on CIrrus.  We get testing in PRs sharing the same automation as the release.  The task "re-run" button can stand in for `workflow_dispatch`.  AWS has a secrets-store service don't they?  I think I remember reading about that.

> If possible I'd like to incrementally improve on these aspects in subsequent PRs

Yep, I'm on-board with that.

Ooops sorry missed a couple notes you had:

> > WSL fails without nested virt
> 
> Ugh..right, that 😞
> 
> > EC2 requires you buy a high end bare metal node
> 
> We have those! Check out the podman_machine task in `.cirrus.yml` 😁

Ah right I did see that land, I wasn't sure how often it was being used. I should sync with you and others off-list on the project funding we can use for windows runs. Windows licensing rates on those bare metal nodes also adds 50%+ the Linux ones. It's around 6 an hour (vs 10 cents if that az feature lands in cirrus - fingers crossed)

One other related but different factor. Depending on how far we want to test, if we try to cover the WSL install case in the installer, it also has to do a system restart mid process (unfortunately requirement of enabling WSL), and thats not CI runner friendly, so we might need to define a task which does a custom remote launch of a VM to cover this. We could also just start with testing pre-installed thats the easier case.
 
>  In fact, maybe this is the reason to do this all on CIrrus. We get testing in PRs sharing the same automation as the release. The task "re-run" button can stand in for `workflow_dispatch`. AWS has a secrets-store service don't they? I think I remember reading about that.

The Azure vault usage doesn't tie us to GHA, we can use it from cirrus, only concern would be the client secrets leaking issue.  (To answer the question though AWS does have one but it doesn't have equivalent tool chain support for windows artifact signing with a remote vault)

I think no mater what we are going to end up with two job contexts, one for PRs and one for building releases, since we can't do signing in PRs due to untrusted code.  The current impl is designed to support that, I tried to keep nearly all of the work outside of the CI definitions so we can move it / share it easily.

> > If possible I'd like to incrementally improve on these aspects in subsequent PRs
> 
> Yep, I'm on-board with that.

Cool. I have been largely working from a goal of what gets this going now and is compatible with the existing process. Also even though there is some new windows specific build, trying to compartmentalize that from the main build system. As an example, it doesn't try to be a competing build for podman.exe

> After an initial look, I'm happy to see this all come together and believe it will be a great benefit to the community. Thanks for taking this work on. I like how you've engineered an "anti-woopsie" button into the workflow, where things can be twiddled with after a release tag is pushed and/or if artifacts are manually built and uploaded.
> 
> My main concerns are relating to the longevity of the automation, especially the batch scripts. The design of github-actions doesn't make this easy, always running the workflow from main but using scripts from the target (tag or branch) with no way to really test changes in a PR before finding out the run will break as soon as a tag is pushed. At the same time, moving this to CIrrus-CI raises some certificate/key/signing safety concerns. Maybe there's a way to do it with a key-store in GCE similar to azure (I don't know)? The biggest reason to have this in Cirrus (IMHO) is team familiarity and that operational changes can be easily validated in a PR before a tag is pushed. Even on an ancient release-branch.

Sure I understand where you are coming from here.  I think we can get the verification aspect you are looking for by adding a PR unsigned build with installer which confirms everything is working (more or less). 

> Lastly, nobody here (besides you) has the experience to debug or troubleshoot errant Windows operations. In the past when unfamiliar automation has been added and the expertise becomes unavailable, at the first sign of breakage it's disabled and forgotten about. That's not your fault, but it would be a real shame given the user base that will benefit from having this working long-term. Anyway, I don't have a good solution here, other than maybe suggesting adding lots more comments and providing links to docs for non-windows experts.

Sure I can provide more comments and documentation. The intention was very much that this build process be robust and isolated enough to require minimal maintenance. Happy to meet interested folks as well to walk through aspects and look at ways to further improve this.

> 
> All that said, maybe this is a good starting place, and some of my long-term/longevity concerns can be addressed in followup PRs. Mainly I just want to ensure we're going into this knowing and aware of some possible "oh crap" scenarios (not that they can/should all be solved right now).

Yeah totally understand. I can start with that PR validation topic and we can sync on any other must haves for now, and collaborate on some future improvement.

@cevich I found some time over the holiday and updated this PR to also include a Cirrus task that verifies the installer builds and runs correctly (sans the WSL elements). // cc @rhatdan @mheon @TomSweeneyRedHat - just letting you know there is an additional commit after you looked at it.
Rebased PR to resolve conflicts from upstream changes

> include a Cirrus task that verifies the installer builds and runs correctly 

Cool!  Taking a look now.

> > I have no knowledge of Windows, and can only do the most cursory of reviews. Minor comments inline. Also, please squash at least some of your commits: there's no reason for a "review feedback" separate commit, and the final ("build/verify") commit has a lot of stuff unrelated to build/verify. Maybe build/verify deserves a separate commit (I can't judge that), but the win-installer changes should be folded into the previous commit. (No comment on the sshproxy commit, I don't know enough)
> 
> @edsantiago Thanks. TBH any assumptions you have on Windows will probably be close enough.
> 
> As to squashing. Can definitely do that, the commits were separate in an attempt to make it easier to review. To your point, though, some of the incremental changes in the last commit should probably have been moved into the second.

Everything is squashed into 3 commits now for review
1. adding win-sshproxy to the release zips
2. the installer build system + gh action
3. the cirrus work to call it
@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL  This should be good to go (unless any additional concerns/reviews), all review comments were followed up on. @cevich and @mheon have LGTM'd
